data.title: Comparing NuScale Power Vs. NANO Nuclear Energy, I Will Stay Away From This Industry
data.description:
Introduction

The bigger the CapEx gets for hyperscalers, the more chips Nvidia (NVDA) sells, and Broadcom (AVGO) helps create. The more chips are coming out to the market, the more data centers are being built. The more data centers are built, the faster we are arriving at the next bottleneck for AI development - power. And it seems like the discussion of this fast-approaching shortage has loomed over the market for a couple of years now, but at the same time, energy has not been the market’s favorite commodity since 2022, according to Bloomberg.

But we are sure that the shortage will happen because there are many metrics that point in that direction. Data center electricity demand estimations vary from firm to firm, although they all agree on one thing: the demand is growing. Supply, at the same time, is not, mostly because of the inability to ramp up electricity production, but more importantly, delivery. It means it’s physically impossible to deliver much more power with the grid that we have today. Even if, let’s say, tomorrow we found a perfect solution for generating lots of electricity out of thin air, the grid would be a bottleneck that would take years to fix.

For this reason, many new, young companies focus either on renewable energy (which can be installed right near data centers to power them directly) or on other various solutions, including Small Modular Reactors. Today, I will be breaking down the latter for the first time. I am not a physicist, but I want to understand why this development is important, what exact needs it can cover, and how to find the best player in this industry. For this, I decided to compare NANO Nuclear Energy Inc. (NNE) with NuScale Power Corporation (SMR), as I was convinced they are direct competitors.

Jumping ahead, after the research, I realized that comparing them is like comparing a commercial airline with a private jet startup - possible, but it makes little sense. At the same time, I would not have understood this if I hadn’t looked at the tech characteristics of each product, and I think it’s important knowledge for each investor in the sector. So today, we will focus mostly on the developments, use cases, clients, and potential, and less on numbers.

Nuclear Power

Nuclear energy is one of the most promising solutions for powering data centers as of today. I’ve found various interesting studies on the matter, but I’d like to highlight the one by the International Atomic Energy Agency because it is focused on the reliability and resilience of the electrical grid when combined with nuclear plants.

I won’t quote the paper because it often gets too technical for our investment goals, but I’ll try to explain the main idea of it: We have low-inertia and high-inertia electricity sources. Low-inertia sources include most of the renewable energy sources, for example, solar panels, wind farms, etc. The rapid increase in the number of those reduces the predictability of generation and system inertia, which leads to changes in system frequencies. Nuclear power plants (NPPs), at the same time, can resist those changes for their high-inertia characteristics, and this stability will be more and more valuable going forward. At the same time, NPPs and the grid have partnership relations - the grid by itself must be stable; otherwise, it can lead to a LOOP (Loss of Off-Site Power) event, but it’s another story, not related to this analysis.

So after reading the previous paragraph, you won’t be surprised to learn that the nuclear power capacity is expected to grow rapidly over the next few years. There are various discussions that nuclear may become the main generator of power for data centers; for example, this Bloomberg chart below shows how many forecasted additions we will have in the next few years of nuclear capacity in the world in the next few years.

While it is impressive, it looks like the vast majority of that capacity is going to China - they are building 120 GW, and this is almost as much as the next 9 countries on the list build together. The U.S. is not even close to the beginning of the list, with only 8 GW of newly added capacity (the U.S. is the leader in absolute terms at the moment, with about 97 GW of operational capacity).

This is very important information for every investor, as when you see those charts and predictions for nuclear power capacity rapid growth in the next few years, you have to ask yourself if you will at all be able to participate in the rally if you don’t invest in Chinese or other countries' companies. The market may be sending American companies up as the shortage gets real, but it doesn’t look like the U.S. will be rapidly growing in this area.

At the same time, the positive characteristics of nuclear plants I’ve discussed before are mostly relevant for the standard big plants and not Small Modular Reactors (SMRs), and I wasn’t able to find any hard data to justify the predictions for growth in this specific segment of the market. Some predictions are very bullish, with a CAGR of 23%+. While it may indeed materialize, at the same time, considering the fact that most companies are years away from their first approved product, these growth estimations look too optimistic for my taste. But let’s now take a look at each of the companies I wanted to break down and compare them.

NuScale Power

NuScale is the first and only company to receive NCR approval of the design of its SMR, although no Small Modular Reactors have been built or are operational in the United States. While two NuScale designs are approved, the first planned commercial project was cancelled, and newer projects are still in the early construction or licensing phases, meaning they do not yet have permission to build those, even though the designs are approved:

The NRC completed a rigorous review of NuScale’s Design Certification Application (DCA), culminating in the issuance of the Final Safety Evaluation Report in August 2020 and the Standard Design Approval in September 2020. This approval confirmed the safety and reliability of NuScale’s 50-MW SMR design and marked a major milestone, both for the company and for advancing clean nuclear technology. The review process took 41 months, setting a record as the fastest design review in the NRC’s history.

In the meantime, another company, Holtec, has gotten the approval for the construction of its SMR-300 units, even before the design approval, because it has chosen a two-step licensing process that allows it to apply for a Construction Permit (CP) based on a preliminary design rather than a fully certified design. Whether this will be a winning strategy, we will have to wait to see, but the important part of this is that the bureaucracy is the main risk for those firms in the near term, at least in the U.S.

What is interesting about Nuscale’s product is that its technology is based on Light Water Reactors (LWRs). This is critical for investors to understand because LWRs are the standard technology used in the vast majority of existing large-scale nuclear plants today. It means that the only product that has ever been approved in this industry is not some new exotic miracle of physics. It’s a smaller version of something that was approved and operating for decades. It is not a bad thing for NuScale, but we will remember this fact when we discuss NANO Nuclear Energy.

NuScale creates power plants, which consist of power modules. Each module generates about 77 MWe. A standard plant might have 6 or 12 of these modules. Remembering the "inertia" concept from the IAEA report, NuScale’s modules are large enough to provide that stabilizing inertia to the grid, although it’s of course much weaker than a regular plant. Because they use Light Water technology, they use much more water than the advanced designs, so they would fit well where coal plants used to be.

Talking of their financial state, the most significant financial development for the quarter was the massive increase in liquidity. NuScale ended Q3 with $753.8 million in cash and equivalents, from $489.9 million the previous quarter. This was driven primarily by an aggressive share issuance in which the company sold 13.2 million Class A shares, generating $475.2 million. While dilution is usually not a good piece of news for existing shareholders, it’s the only way this company will be able to survive until 2030 until real revenue is expected to come.

This increase was driven by the sale of 13.2 million NuScale Class A shares through an at-the-market program during the third quarter, which generated $475.2 million in gross proceeds. Partially offsetting this increase was $128.5 million payment in relation to the PMA milestone triggered by the recent landmark agreement announced by TVA and ENTRA1.

Already now, NuScale reported revenue of $8.2 million (up over 1,600% year-over-year - ah, the growth of the new companies, right?), primarily driven by engineering services for the RoPower project in Romania. However, the company reported a significant EPS loss of -$1.85. A major factor in the company's cash outflow was a $128.5 million payment made to their partner, ENTRA1. This is part of a "Partnership Milestone Agreement" (PMA) designed to accelerate the 6 GW deployment with the Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA).

The company also announced an additional exit for its majority investor, Fluor. Fluor will convert its Class B units into Class A common stock to sell on the market. NuScale says that this will be a "controlled program" to preserve equity value, but I think the stock rightfully plunged on the news. In exchange for this, Fluor agreed to waive certain economic rights, including claims under a tax receivables agreement, which improves NuScale's long-term economic position despite the near-term shift in its shareholder base.

Right now, the stock trades much lower than it did throughout 2025, but the news around this company isn’t great. It looks like investors are leaving, insiders are selling, and even though they are the only company with a design approval, no revenues are expected in the next 4 years.

The stock isn’t cheap by any metric, although it can surge on different speculations of the market. I see no real justification for the levels we’ve seen in 2025, but they may repeat themselves if this bullish AI rally continues. So if you are in for the game, that’s ok, but based on the fundamentals, there is little to catch here.

NANO Nuclear Energy

NANO Nuclear is very different from NuScale. They are trying to bypass the grid and not rely on it whatsoever by developing microreactors (specifically models named ZEUS and ODIN). These are tiny compared to NuScale, producing anywhere from 1 MW to 20 MW. They also have KRONOS, and they present it as the solution for data centers, but, reading a little about this technology, I am very skeptical that it will ever get approved. They want to connect many small reactors together (about 60, if I understood correctly) to power a data center without needing water or a grid. I have read about several new developments that want to do something similar with less dangerous products, like batteries, and this is usually harder than just connecting them and turning them on.

For now, their microreactors are not even close to design approval, not to mention a combined design with 60 parts. Remembering that the review process for NuScale took 41 months, while the technology wasn’t new, it looks like Nano may not get it until 2030, but the demand is coming way before that. Not an ideal situation to be in.

Their moat, though, is in their business model, as it’s the only company that is trying to become a vertically integrated nuclear company. They have subsidiaries dedicated to fuel (HALEU Energy Fuel) and transportation. They are trying to secure the supply chain for the special High-Assay Low-Enriched Uranium that the advanced reactors need and developing the logistics to physically move nuclear material. The idea is that even if their reactor takes a decade to get approved, they might earn something from selling fuel and transport services to other nuclear companies in the meantime. While this is an interesting niche to take, a company that does fuel delivery to nuclear facilities should not be valued as an SMR company.

NANO Nuclear Energy is a company with zero revenue; their financials are purely about "burn" and "runway." At the same time, their strategy has been surprisingly defensive. By the end of 2025, largely thanks to a private placement, they got almost $580 million. For a company of their size and stage, this is an immense amount of capital, which will be enough to keep going for a few years.

Our cash position increased to approximately $580 million following an October 2025 private placement. We view our strong cash position and proven ability to raise funding at scale as a meaningful differentiator. With current cash on hand and our access to the public capital markets, we are well positioned to accelerate the licensing and commercialization of the KRONOS MMR while maintaining the flexibility to expand our vertical integration through disciplined M&A and potential strategic partnerships.

The company is buying assets with that money, so they are not being spent for nothing. They are buying physical facilities and investing in vertical integration, like their uranium enrichment. They are using their inflated stock valuation to buy the supply chain, which can prove to be a very smart move when the reactor is ready and operational.

Ultimately, both companies are asking investors to be okay with a heavy dilution, but they are different in this as well. NuScale is diluting you for a specific commercial destination that is visible on the horizon, and NANO is diluting you to survive until the approval, and nobody knows how many years that will take.

Conclusion

Comparing NuScale Power and NANO Nuclear Energy shows a major difference in how the nuclear companies are attempting to solve the AI energy crisis.

NuScale is using Light Water Reactor technology, meaning they have accepted a lower ceiling of innovation in exchange for a higher floor of regulatory safety, and according to the approval, it worked. For investors, the risk here is execution. I don't know if they can survive the cash burn and manage the construction logistics to get modules online by 2030.

NANO Nuclear thinks that the grid itself is the problem and that the solution is to bypass it entirely with microreactors. However, the risk here is enormous because they are years away from the design approval, not to mention the construction, client attraction, etc. Their vertical integration (fuel and transport) is an interesting idea, but I am not sure it will be able to meaningfully support Nano.

It looks like the speed of AI is much faster than the speed of nuclear, the same as for the regular grid, and other solutions that are available today, besides renewable energy. Hyperscalers need gigawatts of power now, and neither of these companies can provide that.

If you are looking for exposure to the immediate infrastructure, NuScale is the clearer, albeit diluted, path. But if you believe the future of energy is mobile, and you have the stomach to wait for years, NANO looks like a higher speculative upside. In either case, you aren't investing in today's and not even tomorrow’s revenue, and to be honest, I started breaking these two to pick the one that I like more or even call both a buy, but after this analysis, I decided not to pursue this idea.

data.title: Atlassian Q2 Preview: Is The SaaS Apocalypse Over?
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Atlassian Corporation (TEAM), I reiterated my Buy rating with a price target of $214, as the company beat revenue and earnings estimates in their Q1 FY26 and raised full-year guidance while accelerating cRPO (Current Remaining Performance Obligations) and momentum with Rovo.

Unfortunately, I have been terribly wrong with my bullish ratings on Atlassian through all of 2025, as I underestimated the magnitude of multiple contractions that took place with the company’s stock as well as in the entire SaaS complex.

Most recently, with the launch of Anthropic's (ANTHRO) Claude Cowork on January 12, the SaaS complex is going through yet another apocalypse, amid fears that its “seat-based” pricing model will be rendered obsolete by Agentic AI.

While many software companies, including Atlassian, are utilizing a hybrid pricing model that includes both a “seat-based” subscription and a “usage-based” consumption model, investors are not convinced. That is because, unlike the "infrastructure" layer, the software complex is yet to see a meaningful reacceleration in revenue. This applies to Atlassian as well.

The company is scheduled to report its Q2 FY26 earnings on 02/05. However, until investors get clarity on how Agentic AI reshapes traditional SaaS, we may not see a sustained improvement in the company’s forward earnings multiple, which currently sits below the sector median, even when its earnings are expected to grow in the high teens over the next few years with no meaningful downward revision.

While the stock is terribly oversold with a potential short-term rebound of 21%, I will remain on the sidelines and instead consolidate my position in ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW), which I have earlier discussed.

Brief Recap Of Q1 FY26 Earnings

When Atlassian reported its Q1 FY26 earnings at the end of October, revenue grew 21% YoY to $1.46B, driven by Cloud revenue growing by 26% YoY, as well as a 160 basis point benefit from the impact of the Data Center EOL revenue recognition announcement.

Plus, earlier in September, management had also announced plans to EOL (end-of-life) their Data Center products over a multi-year period while introducing their “Atlassian Ascend” program to spearhead their customers’ migration journey from the Data Center to the Cloud to unlock the full potential of their “enterprise-grade” platform.

During the quarter, Atlassian also advanced its “System of Work” product innovation roadmap with “Collections,” where the idea is to move away from standalone apps to a curated set of apps and agents that are built on the Atlassian Platform. In addition to their Teamwork Collection, which includes Jira, Confluence, and Loom, along with their AI solution, Rovo, they also introduced Service and Software Collections, leading to a larger number of multi-year commitments and cRPO accelerating on a sequential basis to 30%.

For the full year, management raised its revenue guidance higher by 280 basis points to 20.8%, driven by Cloud revenue outlook, a greater volume of migrations from the Data Center to Cloud, and an expected benefit of 320 basis points from the Data Center EOL revenue recognition impact. When it comes to profitability, management also raised guidance for gross and operating margins to 86.5% (up 100 basis points) and 25.5% (up 150 basis points), respectively, driven primarily by a 200 basis point tailwind from their Data Center EOL revenue recognition impact.

Putting The State Of Current Software Apocalypse In Context

However, the SaaS complex has been under immense pressure in 2026 so far. This includes companies like Atlassian, ServiceNow, Salesforce, Inc. (CRM), and monday.com Ltd. (MNDY) that are down over 15%+, hugely underperforming the S&P 500 Index (SP500).

While the SaaS complex also struggled in 2025, the latest round of sell-off has been triggered by Anthropic's launch of its Claude Cowork on January 12, 2026. Essentially, Claude Cowork is an autonomous AI agent that can manage files, generate documents from notes, create spreadsheets, and organize workflows without human intervention. This resulted in investor concern that with AI agents handling a wide range of tasks autonomously, companies will now need fewer human workers and therefore fewer software licenses, which threatens the traditional “seat-based” predictable ARR model of software companies.

As Shay Boloor, Chief Market Strategist at Futurum Equities, pointed out below,

“Seat-based pricing is a proxy for labor, so if your revenue model depends on how many humans are doing the work, then you are implicitly betting that digital output scales by adding people. Agentic AI breaks that assumption when buyers believe outcomes can scale without seats, the premium multiple collapses first, long before revenue does.

That’s why nearly every CRM has gone from 40x FCF in 2023 to ~16–18x today. The market isn’t saying these products have no value. It’s saying the old valuation logic can’t be trusted anymore.”

Essentially, what we are seeing, therefore, is a massive repricing in enterprise SaaS companies.

Having said that, there have been a few exceptions in the software complex that have shielded themselves from the massacre.

As I discussed in my previous post on MongoDB: Following Palantir’s Playbook, the reason why both these companies have outperformed at a time when the entire SaaS complex is under a repricing attack is that they positioned themselves as essential “AI infrastructure” plays rather than “AI-disruptible” software applications, while simultaneously benefiting from consumption-based business models.

Does that mean it is time to dump traditional SaaS companies like Atlassian?

Aaron Levie, CEO at Box, put out this thoughtful post on X, where he argued that the proliferation of AI agents will expand the size of software markets massively. This is how he frames it below.

“I’d argue that in a world of 100X more AI agents than people in an enterprise, the value of the systems of record and tools agents will use will go up, not down. Because in this new world, software provides the guardrails on which agents can operate successfully within an enterprise and gives them the underlying tools to use to be more productive themselves and work alongside people.

For instance, if you ask an AI agent to generate 10X the number of outbound email and marketing messages, where will all those agents find the data to work with, and where will the leads end up going to have a human sales rep act on them? Almost certainly some form of a CRM system. Or when an AI Agent processes reviews invoices and transactions, where will they enter this data when they’re done to kick off a supply chain workflow? Again, an existing or reinvented ERP system. As long as we have humans and agents interacting with one another, there will need to be deterministic software that bridges these interactions seamlessly.

And as a result of all of these new agentic users within our software systems, the size of the enterprise software market is going to increase massively.”

At the same time, he also points out that the business model of software companies will have to evolve from “seat-based” pricing to “consumption/usage-based” pricing. To be fair, we are already seeing that happening with several software companies, including Atlassian, where they are integrating Agentic AI and monetizing it through a “consumption-based” pricing model.

For instance, in Atlassian’s case, the company utilizes a hybrid model with a mix of premium subscriptions that give enterprises access to its core AI agents as well as usage-based credits for its Rovo platform.

What is also important to note is that the market may be overestimating the speed of disruption and completely discounting the possibility that existing SaaS players can successfully adapt and compete in Agentic AI.

However, unlike the revenue bump that we have seen in the "infrastructure" layer, traditional SaaS is yet to demonstrate meaningful signs of AI monetization. As Shay Boloor points out,

“Until investors have clarity on who captures pricing power when outcomes scale without seats, money stays tight, multiples stay compressed, and software trades like a sector in exile.”

What To Expect in Q2 FY26?

Atlassian is scheduled to report its Q2 FY26 earnings on 02/05, where revenue and earnings per share are expected to grow at 20.03% and 19.00% YoY during the quarter.

As per consensus estimates, both revenue and earnings per share are expected to accelerate in the following two quarters.

However, as Citi analyst Fatima Boolani pointed out, their Data Center EOL announcement has complicated their financial model mechanics, which in turn is boosting FY26 revenue outlook but creating tougher comps for FY27. However, what is even more concerning, according to the analyst, is the quality and cadence of topline growth, which she expects to be weak outside of pricing increases, migration from Data Center to Cloud, and accounting impacts.

As we can see below, forward revenue growth expectations remain muted, with analysts projecting no lift as such from AI monetization.

At this point, in order for Atlassian stock to regain investor confidence, I believe a simple beat and raise won’t do. Instead, management will have to demonstrate meaningful traction across both its “seat-based” and “usage-based” pricing models, which points to faster-than-expected revenue growth in FY26 and acceleration beyond that.

Assessing The Investment Thesis & Conclusions

With the SaaS complex getting massively repriced as its “seat-based” recurring ARR comes under attack, it is important to note that on a 6M basis, there have been no meaningful downward revisions to Atlassian’s forward revenue and earnings growth estimates.

However, with no signs of revenue reacceleration ahead, investors are convinced that the entire SaaS complex is dead and incapable of adapting and competing.

Yet in all of this, one may argue that a forward revenue growth in the high teens is quite strong, while Atlassian has been innovating on its Agentic AI product roadmap with Rovo to redefine the company’s “System of Work” vision. In its Q1 FY26 earnings, monthly active users of Rovo grew 50% QoQ to 3.5M, with over 2.4M business workflows orchestrated through Rovo as of late 2025.

At the moment, Atlassian is trading at a forward P/E ratio of just 24, which is lower than the sector median. In fact, over the last year, its forward PE ratio has contracted by over 70%, which is terribly disappointing, especially when its earnings per share are projected to grow in the high teens range over the next 2-3 years, as can be seen below.

This forward P/E ratio gets even further compressed to a little over 17 when taking projected FY28 earnings per share into account.

Like I said earlier, until investors get further clarity into how Agentic AI reshapes traditional SaaS, the P/E ratio may remain depressed for longer than one may expect.

However, given that the daily RSI is 19, the stock is terribly oversold at current levels and should stage a rebound to its 10-day moving average (which is trending lower) to $143 per share, which represents an upside of 21% from current levels.

So, while short-term investors can play for a rebound, I will remain on the sidelines with Atlassian until we get clarity and instead consolidate my enterprise SaaS position in my highest conviction name, that is, ServiceNow.

data.title: Microsoft: Something Doesn't Add Up
data.description:
Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) reported a solid fiscal first quarter in 2026, beating both top and bottom line expectations by 3.03% and 12.73%, respectively. But, despite the strong momentum, Microsoft's stock has dropped ~15% since the earnings release. This logic doesn't add up.

I agree, most of the setback has stemmed from fears about AI spending and sustainability, since Microsoft is one of those that are betting heavily on AI infrastructure. Even so, the fundamentals haven’t changed, and there are still several factors that point to continued momentum in 2026.

For those reasons, my Strong Buy stance for Microsoft remains in place—despite the secular headwinds.

Microsoft’s fiscal first-quarter 2026 earnings recap

While Microsoft's second quarter numbers are due out on January 28, 2025, for context, I thought I'd include this little recap of what happened in Q1.

In the most recent quarter, Microsoft’s revenue rose 18% to $77.7 billion, driven by its Intelligent Cloud segment, which contributed $30.9 billion alone to the top line. On that subject, this segment’s revenue increased 28% compared to the same period last year. Within that sphere, Azure grew 40% in constant currency, with AI services (Dynamics 365) delivering ~18 percentage points to that growth. This tells me that customers are indeed pouring money into AI integration as I write this.

In terms of profitability, gross profit rose 18% to $53.6 billion, while gross margin stayed relatively flat at ~69%. That’s impressive considering that the company is building data centers at an unusually high pace. Meanwhile, operating income climbed 24% to $37.9 billion, and net income rose 12% to $27.7 billion, or $3.73 per share. That said, the bottom line figures are a tad lower as Microsoft took a $3.1 billion charge related to its OpenAI (OPENAI) investment.

Looking at the balance sheet, the company ended the quarter with $102 billion in cash and short-term investments against a long-term debt of $35.4 billion. Zooming out, Microsoft’s total assets reached $636.3 billion with total liabilities of ~$270 billion. That’s a pretty healthy financial position.

Speaking of cash, its cash flow paints an even brighter picture. Operating cash flow for the quarter increased 32% to $45 billion, while free cash flow jumped 33% to $25.7 billion. So despite record spending on infrastructure, cash generation is actually accelerating.

For Q2, management is guiding revenue to come in between $79.5 and $80.6 billion, up between 14% and 16% YoY. Also, Intelligent Cloud revenue is expected to come in between $32.25 and $32.55 billion, or ~27% higher year-over-year. Meanwhile, Azure is expected to grow 37% in constant currency. If any of this materializes, it should more than help clear up uncertainties around AI computing demand.

Of course, CapEx is also expected to increase from here, exceeding that of fiscal 2025 growth rates, and more importantly, computing capacity remains limited through at least the end of the fiscal year.

How is MSFT stock performing?

Microsoft stock currently trades at ~$460 per share at the time of this publication. Over the past six months, MSFT stock is down roughly ~9%, while it's up ~8% on a one-year scale. Zoom out five years, and the stock is up ~113%, mainly driven by its cloud infrastructure business.

In terms of valuation, Microsoft trades at a FWD price-to-book ratio of roughly 8x, which is about 60% higher than the sector median. As a long-term shareholder myself, I’d agree that Microsoft’s real value comes from its relationships with practically every single Fortune 500 company and its dominant position in the AI market—so this premium makes sense.

However, the company’s FWD P/E ratio (GAAP) sits at ~29x, which is around 8% lower than the sector median—despite being a notably dominant player in AI and cloud computing. At this point, Microsoft is the cheapest in the Magnificent 7 lineup, considering traditional valuation metrics:

To me, this 8% discount reflects short-term worries around AI spending—not the fundamentals. As you’ve seen from its financials, there’s really nothing to worry about Microsoft’s current standing.

Moreover, the company has been building AI infrastructure at an incredible pace. Once these data centers fill up, there’ll be a clear path to margin expansion. On that subject, Microsoft’s current levels might be a good entry point for investors with a five-year or longer horizon. In the short term, it’s certainly possible Microsoft could continue to decline as the market reacts to AI spending news or Bubble Bursting rumors. But again, I think this is very short term.

What are Microsoft’s potential growth drivers in 2026?

Several factors could boost Microsoft’s trajectory over the next 12 months, each with the potential to bring in billions in revenue, though they’ll play out at different times. The most immediate one being…

AI infrastructure demand surge

Again, Azure AI has grown by 40% compared to the same period last year, but this figure actually understates demand. If you look deeper, Microsoft’s commercial backlog increased 51% to $392 billion—these are signed contracts with customers who’ve committed to spending on Azure. On average, the contract duration is about two years, meaning this backlog will turn into actual revenue in a relatively short period of time.

Management’s response to this strong demand is doubling its data center footprint over the next two years. One of these projects is the Fairwater campus in Wisconsin, which is expected to have 2GW of power capacity by itself. Across everything it's building, Microsoft plans to add more than 80% AI capacity this fiscal year alone, and the total installed base is doubling by fiscal 2028.

CEO Satya Nadella’s exact statements were:

We will increase our total AI capacity by over 80% this year and roughly double our total data center footprint over the next 2 years, reflecting the demand signals we see. Just this quarter, we announced the world's most powerful AI data center, Fairwater in Wisconsin, which will go online next year and scale to 2 gigawatts alone.

But how will this expansion potentially impact the company’s bottom line over the following years? Remember, Azure is growing at 40% now, but that pace will eventually slow as the law of large numbers kicks in. A more reasonable rate might be 25% annually as the business matures further.

At 25% annual growth from today’s levels, reaching $1.54 trillion in revenue would take about 6 to 7 years. So, we're potentially looking at a path to 5x net income by 2031, assuming Microsoft keeps its margins steady as the infrastructure investments pay off.

Capacity expansion coming online

Another potential growth driver is the capacity expansion itself. Nadella’s remarks about the 80% increase in AI capacity for fiscal 2026 are significant. In the first fiscal quarter alone, capital spending reached $34.9 billion, which puts the company on track for nearly $140 billion in annual spending. All that money is going towards bringing new data centers online quarter after quarter.

Although many are concerned about the spending levels—and rightfully so—CFO Amy Hood was really transparent about this matter. She flat-out said that Azure revenue would be higher if Microsoft just had enough capacity to serve everyone in line. Her exact remarks were:

It’s always hard to quantify precisely what would have been the revenue impact in quarter, but I would offer a way to think about it, is Azure probably does bear most of the revenue impact, because when you think about real priorities that you have to fill first,” “And yes, it does impact Azure directly. That is the place where you see that prioritization, but I think it’s probably hard for me to give an exact number. But it is safe to say that the number could be higher.

To me, these constraints are holding back Azure growth by a notable margin. A modest 5 to 8% bottleneck would translate to $4 to $6 billion in quarterly revenue sitting on the table because Microsoft literally can’t cater to everyone. These figures are pretty conservative considering how much Microsoft is building to address the demand.

Throughout fiscal 2026 and into 2027, new data centers are expected to come online, so all that pent-up demand should start converting into actual revenue.

More importantly, Nvidia’s GB300 GPU clusters will be deployed in these new data centers, and this will be Microsoft’s first large-scale deployment of the said GPUs. According to the report, these new chips deliver about 30% more performance per chip than older models, which means Microsoft can serve more customer work for every dollar spent in these data centers.

If everything goes right, this expansion could easily add 2 to 3% to annual revenue growth through 2026 and beyond as supply limitations ease. From 2027 to 2028, we might start seeing operating margins expand by up to 2% per year from these investments alone and contribute significantly to the $138.5 billion quarterly net income target.

OpenAI partnership strengthened

Another potential growth opportunity for Microsoft is its restructured deal with OpenAI—it might even be its most important strategic move in the AI business. For starters, Microsoft owns ~27% of OpenAI at a valuation of ~$500 billion. That’s nearly a 10x return on the ~$13.8 billion that Microsoft invested in OpenAI. Now, OpenAI has committed to buying $250 billion worth of Azure services on top of what they’re already spending.

Why is this important? Well, the agreement gives Microsoft exclusive commercial licensing rights to OpenAI’s technology through 2032 or at least until AGI, or Artificial General Intelligence, is verified. AGI is basically AI that can do intellectual tasks as well as a human can.

That said, AGI is still theoretical. Microsoft’s exclusivity means that any company that wants to build apps using OpenAI models must run them on Azure. So, Microsoft pretty much controls the distribution channel for the world’s top AI models so far.

That $250 billion commitment will likely be spread over 5 to 7 years, working out to about $35 to $50 billion in extra revenue from Azure once it's fully ramped up. Azure’s current gross margins stand at ~70%, so that would translate to $24.5 to $35 billion in annual gross profit.

As for the bottom line, that would be ~$50 billion in added Azure revenue per year if that $250 billion spreads over 5 years. And, at 68% gross margins, you get roughly $34 billion in extra gross profit. Let’s say operating expenses scale at ~30% of that revenue as Microsoft builds support/sales teams. That would leave ~$19 billion in extra operating income, or roughly $15 billion additional net income after taxes, given the company keeps its gross margins stable.

My verdict on Microsoft

To me, Microsoft is at a turning point where short-term challenges have overshadowed a strong long-term opportunity. Current fears around AI spending and sustainability, Microsoft’s capacity constraints, and limited movement in gross margins have all contributed to the stocks’ ~15% decline since earnings, but most of these are fixable issues or non-fundamental setbacks.

I agree that the risk-reward favors building a position here for investors with at least a 5-year horizon. Of course, expect share price volatility as quarterly results bounce around based on capacity and market reaction towards headlines.

With everything outlined above, my Strong Buy rating for Microsoft remains unchanged.

data.title: Oracle: Sell Before The AI Bubble Bursts
data.description:
The depreciation of Oracle Corporation's (ORCL) shares has been significant in recent months, but it is unlikely to be over. While the company was able to greatly increase its remaining performance obligations in the last two quarters, there's a risk that the business itself will get limited returns from the AI-related deals that it signed in 2025. At the same time, as the overall market sentiment slowly changes and the risk premium for AI-related stocks rises, there's a risk that Oracle's stock could lose even more value and disappoint investors in 2026.

The Sentiment Is Shifting

Oracle made headlines in September after it signed a 5-year, $300 billion deal with OpenAI under which it will supply the computing power to the creator of ChatGPT. As a result of this, Oracle's remaining performance obligations surged, its stock jumped to new highs, and the company's founder, Larry Ellison, at one point became the world's richest person. However, despite all the optimism surrounding the deal, Oracle's stock nevertheless began to lose traction shortly after reaching new highs and has lost all the value that it previously gained since the deal with OpenAI was signed.

One of the biggest catalysts behind the latest depreciation is the shift in sentiment and the change in perception of AI-related businesses by the market. The MIT study from last year showed that 95% of organizations currently get zero returns on their AI investments, while one of HSBC's latest reports indicates that OpenAI itself is unlikely to become profitable by the end of this decade. As a result, some investors have become skeptical about the ability of OpenAI to commit around $1 trillion to deals that it signed with companies like Oracle and others, especially since it's expected to report around a $10 billion loss for fiscal 2025.

That's why it's not a surprise that investors in recent months started to look for hedging instruments in case the overall AI-related spending comes to a halt if some big companies or hyperscalers are unable to fulfill their parts of the deal. The fact that the spread for Oracle's credit default swaps in recent months reached the highest level since the Great Recession shows that the risk of an AI bubble that could pop at any moment is real. Therefore, even Oracle's successful Q2 earnings report, which was released last month and showed that the revenues and the remaining performance obligations increased, didn't stop the depreciation of the company's shares.

More importantly, to fulfill its part of the deal, Oracle would need to commit significant resources to buy the land and build the data centers, which will be able to provide computing power to its customers. To get those resources, Oracle would likely be prompted to reach out to the debt market if it wants to build the expensive data centers. The reason why we're thinking that debt financing is one of the few options that Oracle has is that the company started to significantly burn cash in recent months. During the Q1 earnings call in September, the company noted that its free cash flow in the last four quarters was negative $5.9 billion. Then, during the Q2 earnings call in December, the management stated that in the latest quarter alone, the free cash flow was a negative $10 billion. This shows that its business can no longer cover the existing expenditures that remain significant and continue to rise. For a company that has a debt-to-equity ratio of over 500% and a net debt position of $111.96 billion at the end of last quarter, this is a problem. If FCF remains negative in the foreseeable future, which is likely given the rising CapEx, Oracle might reach a point when it will become much harder for it to service all that debt.

Also, considering that the company's own report from October showed that the business of renting computing power to others is challenging and has thin margins, there's a risk that after building all the data centers, the return on their investments will be limited. What's worse is that even if the margins issue is resolved at the beginning, a significant amount of resources that are allocated to building data centers are spent on buying powerful GPUs. Considering that the lifespan of the latest GPUs is mostly up to six years, while more powerful chips are expected to be released quite frequently, there's a risk that Oracle will be left with obsolete AI accelerators fairly quickly and won't be able to generate decent returns.

That's why it's not a surprise that the market's optimism has quickly faded, and Oracle's shares have been mostly depreciating since late September. The fact that the company became a target of a class action lawsuit last week due to the AI-related losses just shows that investors are losing confidence in Oracle's ability to generate any decent returns from its business of providing computing power to its customers.

The Real Value of Oracle

With the amount of challenges that Oracle is facing, we believe that its shares still have room to fall, and we created a valuation model to figure out the company's intrinsic value. In the model, we used a 3% perpetual growth rate, which is the same rate as we use most of the time when figuring out the value of other companies. The rate is also similar to the historical GDP and inflation rate. Considering that the perpetual rate basically refers to the growth rate at which the company will grow indefinitely, giving a greater rate makes no sense since under such a scenario, at one point, the business will be worth more than the overall economy, which is not a realistic possibility.

The data for the cash reserves and debt were taken from the recent earnings report.

The discount rate in the model is 8.58%, which we figured out after calculating Oracle's cost of debt and cost of equity. The data for calculating the cost of debt was mostly taken from the trailing twelve months. For the cost of equity, we used a risk-free rate of 4.15%, a beta of 1.57, and a market return rate of 7.84%.

In the forecast table below, our sales assumptions are similar to the overall consensus, while the EBIT margin is expected to gradually improve over time. The tax rate in the model is the same as in the previous year.

As for the CapEx, during the Q1 earnings call, Oracle's management expected the expenditures to reach $35 billion. However, during the Q2 earnings call, the management said that they now expect the expenditures to be $15 billion over the Q1 assumptions. The expenditures are expected to be used on expanding the business to honor its remaining performance in the following years. Considering this, we reflected an increase in CapEx in FY26 and beyond. At the same time, we also increased the assumptions for the D&A due to fairly quick depreciation of GPUs, which are going to account for a significant portion of CapEx. The change in net working capital as a percentage of sales is similar to Oracle's historical performance.

Our model shows that Oracle's enterprise value is $500.99 billion, while its equity value is $389.03 billion. That translates to an intrinsic value of $133.14 per share, which is below the current market price by around 33%.

Risks To Our Bearish Thesis

Despite all the challenges, Oracle nevertheless reaffirmed its guidance last month and could even raise the manageable level of debt to fund its projects, which could make it possible for the company to undermine our bearish thesis.

At the same time, as hyperscalers are expected to spend record sums on scaling their AI capabilities in 2026, while the overall economy is also forecasted to grow at a decent rate this year, there's a possibility that the stock market will continue to grow. This could help Oracle's shares find a solid technical support level and potentially rebound over time.

Final Thoughts

The massive depreciation of Oracle's stock in recent months indicates that the market is questioning the latest deals that the business signed with major AI companies and is not convinced that significant returns from those deals are fully guaranteed. As the overall sentiment surrounding AI stocks is shifting and the risk premium rises, investors might benefit more from buying safer assets in the current environment. Considering that Oracle is also overvalued even after the latest depreciation, we find it hard to see a major rebound of its shares anytime soon. For that reason, we decided to rate Oracle as a Sell.

data.title: Alger Spectra Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Update
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Alger Spectra Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Class A shares of the Alger Spectra Fund underperformed the Russell 3000 Growth Index during the fourth quarter of 2025. Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), Natera, Inc. (NTRA), and Cidara Therapeutics, Inc. (CDTX) were among the top contributors to performance.

Alphabet is the parent of Google and a global leader in digital advertising, cloud computing, and AI-driven consumer services. Shares contributed positively during the quarter as investors increasingly recognized improving execution across the company’s AI strategy, supported by strong engagement trends and rising AI-related workloads. Google Cloud was a key driver, with management highlighting accelerating usage, including increased AI token processing and continued momentum in the Gemini ecosystem. Sentiment was further supported by strengthening cloud demand and new customer wins, as well as reports that Google may broaden access to its TPU capabilities, including potential deployments beyond Google Cloud.

Natera is a specialty diagnostics laboratory that provides high-value genetic testing across three core franchises: reproductive health (including non-invasive prenatal testing for chromosomal conditions such as trisomy 13, 18, and 21), oncology (led by Signatera, which measures circulating tumor DNA to assess treatment response and detect molecular residual disease and cancer recurrence), and transplant (tests used to monitor organ rejection). These tests are built on the company’s proprietary cell-free DNA liquid biopsy platform, enabling highly sensitive detection from a blood sample. Shares contributed positively after the company reported strong fiscal third-quarter earnings, with management commentary pointing to revenue and margin performance that exceeded expectations on the back of higher test volumes and favorable reimbursement dynamics— particularly within the Signatera franchise—alongside another increase to full-year 2025 revenue and margin guidance.

Cidara Therapeutics is a biotechnology company developing drug-Fc conjugate (DFC) therapeutics, with its lead program CD388, an investigational long-acting, strain-agnostic antiviral designed to prevent influenza infection in individuals at higher risk of complications. CD388 was supported by compelling Phase 2b NAVIGATE results and advanced into the Phase 3 ANCHOR study, positioning it as a potentially first-in-class, single-dose prophylactic option for influenza prevention. Share contributed positively after Merck announced an all-cash agreement to acquire Cidara for approximately $9.2 billion.

Our short exposure to a global technology company contributed positively to performance. During the quarter, management issued weaker-than-expected forward earnings guidance and announced a restructuring plan that included meaningful headcount reductions, reinforcing concerns about near-term profitability. Investors also remained skeptical that strength in PCs could offset continued softness in printing. As a result, the company’s share price declined during the quarter, benefiting our short position.

Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), and Meta Platforms Inc. (META) were among the top detractors from performance.

Nebius is a provider of AI-focused cloud infrastructure, operating GPU compute capacity across Europe and expanding in the U.S. We view the company as a differentiated “neocloud” beneficiary of accelerating AI adoption, as demand for high-quality GPU compute continues to outstrip available supply of chips, power, and data center capacity. Nebius already operates a first-party data center in Finland and has deployed GPU capacity in Paris, while adding U.S. capacity through a Kansas City, Missouri deployment and a new data center build-out in New Jersey. During the quarter, shares detracted amid a broader selloff in AI infrastructure-related equities as investors grew more concerned about the “circularity” of AI ecosystem financing and the durability of AI infrastructure spending. Despite near-term volatility, Nebius continues to sign large, long-term agreements with tier-one customers, including multi-year AI infrastructure contracts with major cloud service providers, improving revenue visibility as new capacity comes online.

Microsoft is a beneficiary of corporate America’s transformative digitization. The company operates through three segments: Productivity and Business Processes (Office365, LinkedIn, and Dynamics), Intelligent Cloud (Server Products and Cloud Services, Azure, and Enterprise Services), and More Personal Computing (Windows, Devices, Gaming, and Search). Shares detracted during the quarter after the company reported Azure cloud growth and forward guidance fell short of elevated investor expectations, in part because revenue recognition lagged strong demand and the company remained capacity constrained. While Azure still grew 39% year-over-year (YoY) and management guided to 37% growth in the current quarter, we believe investors focused on the near-term mismatch between demand and available capacity. Importantly, demand signals improved: commercial bookings surged (reported up approximately 111% YoY) and remaining performance obligations rose 51% compared to 37% from the prior quarter, extending revenue visibility. To address the backlog and support AI/cloud workloads, management signaled a step-up in investment, including roughly $30B of capital expenditures (CapEx) in the current quarter and an expectation that fiscal-2026 CapEx growth will be higher than fiscal-2025.

Meta is the world’s largest social-media company, operating platforms that include Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger. Shares detracted during the quarter as investors focused on management’s guidance for materially higher operating expenses and capital expenditures (CapEx) tied to AI infrastructure investments. Management noted that 2026 CapEx is expected to be “notably” higher than 2025, which pressured forward earnings and free-cash-flow expectations despite strong underlying results. While the company highlighted continued advertising momentum and indicated that revenue growth could remain robust into year-end, investors remained concerned about the magnitude and timing of the AI-related CapEx.

Our short exposure to a system integrator of utility-scale battery storage detracted from performance. During the quarter, the company reported mixed fiscal fourth-quarter results, with revenue missing expectations due to ongoing domestic manufacturing ramp- up issues. Despite the revenue miss, the company posted stronger- than-expected margins and delivered a record quarter for orders and backlog, prompting management to initiate fiscal 2026 revenue guidance that investors viewed as more feasible given substantial backlog support. Management also pointed to an emerging data center opportunity, citing a largely new behind-the-meter pipeline and an expanding total addressable market that could contribute more meaningfully over the longer term. The stock rose on these developments, pressuring our short position during the quarter.

The first full calendar year that Fred Alger Management, LLC was the Fund’s investment manager was 1975; therefore, since inception performance is reflected as of this date. The Fund operated as a closed end fund from August 23, 1978 to February 12, 1996. The calculation of total return during that time assumes dividends were reinvested at market value. Had dividends not been reinvested, performance would have been lower. On September 24, 2008, the Fund’s name was changed from Spectra Fund to Alger Spectra Fund, and the Fund’s Class N shares were redesignated as Class A shares. data.title: Alger Spectra Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Strategy

Primarily invests in growth equity securities of U.S. companies identified through our fundamental research as demonstrating promising growth potential, and engages in short selling (up to approximately 10% of the market value of the portfolio). Seeks long-term capital appreciation.

Highlights

During the fourth quarter of 2025, the largest portfolio sector weightings were Information Technology and Communication Services. The largest sector overweight was Communication Services and the largest sector underweight was Health Care.

The Information Technology and Utilities sectors contributed to relative performance while Health Care and Communication Services were among sectors that detracted from relative performance.

Market Environment

U.S. equities ended the fourth quarter on a firm footing, with the S&P 500 Index returning 2.7%, extending the steady grind upward that characterized 2025, after the tariff announcements. Investor confidence was supported by a familiar trio: corporate earnings that came in better than expected, a Federal Reserve (Fed) that pushed further into an easing stance, and a macroeconomic backdrop that — despite mounting cross-currents — remained resilient in the aggregate. Market sentiment also benefited from improving clarity on trade policy, including an extension of the U.S. – China truce and selective tariff relief aimed at easing consumer affordability pressures.

At the same time, the quarter was defined by widening dispersion beneath the index-level surface. The market’s biggest tailwind — continued investment tied to artificial intelligence (AI) — was increasingly accompanied by scrutiny around AI infrastructure bottlenecks, sources of financing, and whether the AI theme could deliver returns commensurate with the capital deployed. Macroeconomic risks also remained in play: labor market softening became harder to ignore, consumers appeared more cautious and bifurcated across income cohorts, and housing stayed under pressure. Complicating the read-through, a prolonged government shutdown reduced economic data visibility, amplifying debate over inflation and employment signals just as global central-bank dynamics leaned less uniformly supportive. Nevertheless, U.S. equities reinforced a constructive baseline for the S&P 500 Index, where Health Care was the top performing sector, while Real Estate was the weakest.

During the quarter, we continued to observe secular themes that we believe are creating attractive investment opportunities—corporations are digitizing their operations, cloud computing is growing and supporting innovation, and AI is at an inflection point, potentially enabling significant increases in productivity. Moreover, U.S. business spending appears to be accelerating, driven by a confluence of factors—most notably burgeoning demand for AI infrastructure and tax incentives from the One Big Beautiful Bill, which allow companies to deduct 100% of eligible equipment and qualifying manufacturing-facility costs in the year placed in service, boosting near-term cash flow and after-tax returns.

Despite the revenue miss, the company posted stronger- than-expected margins and delivered a record quarter for orders and backlog, prompting management to initiate fiscal 2026 revenue guidance that investors viewed as more feasible given substantial backlog support. Management also pointed to an emerging data center opportunity, citing a largely new behind-the-meter pipeline and an expanding total addressable market that could contribute more meaningfully over the longer term. The stock rose on these developments, pressuring our short position during the quarter.

data.title: AST SpaceMobile: The Story Is Exploding, But Don't Chase The Breakout Yet
data.description:
I thought the bubble on AST SpaceMobile, Inc. (ASTS) would implode to end 2025, but I was wrong. Not only did ASTS defy my wildest imagination (and expectations), but the stock of the space-based cellular broadband network operator surged well >130% since my last ASTS writeup in June 2025. I must say I didn't fathom such a breathtaking riposte to what I thought was a reasonably conceived caution (I indicated a Hold/Neutral rating in case you think I was bearish). More importantly, I didn't acknowledge a Sell position was necessary, given the ascendency of the industry (leveraging off Elon Musk's SpaceX-Starlink's (SPACE) preeminence) and the potential multi-trillion dollar IPO this year that could also see Musk attain the unbelievable trillionaire status even before he achieves the milestones embedded in his recently revised Tesla (TSLA) compensation.

For AST SpaceMobile, the near-term catalysts are almost about making sure that their launch cadence is carefully maintained and executed with little fuss. These are the foundational pillars of its nascent business model that must be executed almost flawlessly in laying the path to achieving 45-60 satellites in orbit by the end of 2026. Competition to help ASTS launch these satellites into space has also heated up recently, and the company indicates that it's taking a long-term view with their contracted providers, while also making sure that they have signed up for additional launches, which could scale to >100 satellites over time.

The market's reaction to these potential catalysts is clearly fast and furious. Incredibly, while there are obviously pretty steep pullbacks along the way, dip buyers have consistently returned to help the stock break into new highs subsequently. That pattern has persisted with unwavering conviction even through this week's highs, as the company announced that it has been a “prime contract” for the “US Missile Defense Agency SHIELD program.”

There were already prior expectations that ASTS could participate in one way or another, as the potential of “dual use tech” is now coming into play, as part of the US's proposed Golden Dome missile defense system. While this doesn't immediately confer a substantive revenue pathway or pipeline (yet) skewing in favor of ASTS's pipeline in the near term, I'm in concurrence with management's assertion that the contract “validates its unique, on-orbit, dual-use technology and our growing capabilities within the defense sector.”

As a note, ASTS had already remarked in December on how AST SpaceMobile is primed to partake, given the array of strategic imperatives that the US government intends to deploy, leveraging on the firm and its leading peers in their core capabilities, while also avoiding the so-called “vendor lock” (presumably or particularly with SpaceX) that the US government is arguably keen to abstain from given the extremely high stakes involved in this massive initiative:

We can do other things with our frequencies, including RADAR, that support a lot of very strategic capabilities for the U.S. government and its allies. So that is what's brought us into the Golden Dome conversation as it's evolved over the past year, where we see -- we had a very positive U.S. government investment space backdrop over the last 5 to 10 years. But with this year, it's really accelerated. There's currently a $150 billion RFP open for a lot of the stuff that's involved and will be involved with Golden Dome over time. These are really big use cases that are really relevant where the government is looking for dual-use capabilities, which means government services, free riding or riding on commercial investment, which is what we are, as well as avoiding vendor lock, which means you're not stuck in a contract with 1 prime for a decade because you've made some investment. You want to dual source it. - AST SpaceMobile presents at UBS Global Media and Communications Conference 2025

Such developments, I believe, should serve to layer on top of the burgeoning source of commercial partnerships that have been struck with an increasingly larger slate of MNO partners. These were highlighted earlier in ASTS's Q3 earnings scorecard. I believe the core notion that is underlining the company's narrative right now isn't so much about whether the tech still needs further validation, but rather whether they can effectively monetize these partnerships quickly as they get those launches into orbit.

Therefore, while the $1B in contracted revenue serves to assure investors of the potential for revenue conversion, execution will still hinge upon the launches and deliveries, as the market rechannels its focus from liquidity (reportedly having $3.2B in pro forma cash and equivalents) and technological capabilities. The milestones enumerated in the Block 2 Bluebird deployment plan will help investors ascertain whether the target could lean closer to the low end of the schedule, or could it skew toward the 60 satellites mark by the end of this year.

Hence, I think it's pivotal for investors to scrutinize the earlier developments in the key markets identified for early 2026, namely in the US, Canada, Japan, Saudi Arabia, and the UK. For astute ASTS investors, I believe they already have an inkling that while the near-term margins could come under pressure, the deployment sequences have afforded the market a semblance of clarity in the near term.

Hence, the focus has decidedly shifted to the potential for long-term earnings accretion, as compared to worrying incessantly about short-term liquidity constraints, which doesn't look like they could hamper ASTS significantly for now, given the substantive liquidity raise it undertook in 2025, which bolstered its cash runway markedly. And the level of operating leverage will very much depend on how quickly management scales its launches and converts the contracted revenue, which should percolate into its bottomline, augmenting its self-sufficiency and reducing the uncertain cash flow variability in its forward outlook:

So when you look through the history of satellite companies the last 20 years, when the businesses have been doing well and growing, you see 85% margins for wholesale businesses. And when you dig into those companies, even today, where there's attractive segments, there can be 90% plus marginal economics, EBITDA margin flow-through margin. So for us, there's a big CapEx upfront. We all know that. That's why we funded the business the way we have. Once it's in orbit, very little cost to maintain. There'll be a small maintenance CapEx line item over time as the 7- to 10-year cycle of the asset is recycled. But our OpEx, which is in the $60 million per quarter today, might trend up a little bit with growth. The opportunities we're seeing are phenomenal. But if you're looking at that plus some lease expense, you still have a pretty fixed margin business. We have some revenue shares, including with the operators, but those will all come out of the -- before we get the gross revenue on our income statement. So the flow-through margin here and the operating leverage is pretty special, and we expect that to be in excess of 90% over time. - AST SpaceMobile presents at UBS Global Media and Communications Conference 2025

So, this is precisely what we see right now, isn't it? Wall Street is clearly baking in a more robust ramp and revenue accretion through 2028 and beyond. Hence, the near-term focus will be to get those satellites into orbit throughout 2026, and then we should see the monetization economies starting to cascade into ASTS's P&L with some patience.

Well, that's (however) obviously easier said than done, as the focus turns undoubtedly to deployment and execution. But I think what the market has also attempted to bake into ASTS's valuation multiples (almost 190x on a forward revenue basis based on enterprise value) is that we should be looking at the stock as an end-of-the-decade free cash flow story.

The current liquidity buffer indicates that ASTS should be well-positioned to maintain its cadence. The participation in the SHIED program corroborates a superior growth optionality that should enhance the thesis of ASTS.

Nevertheless, while I think the stock is seemingly leaning even more bullish with the higher-lows and higher-highs market print reflected on its price action, I'm still somewhat cautious as I want to see how the 2026 deployment schedules proceed first.

And we also know that the stock is inherently volatile, so I'm quite sure that there are opportunities for investors who are thinking about loading up at the next pullback after the current euphoria settles down a little. If I think it's truly worth a shot, I will most certainly inform subscribers first in my Investing Group: Ultimate Growth Investing so that they can also consider taking part in this new growth vector for their portfolio.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

Investment Philosophy:

Purchase durable business franchises that are selling at a discount to their intrinsic value.

Investment Process:

We utilize a business owner's approach to investing, thoroughly investigating the economics of the business and the quality of the management team. Some of the characteristics of good businesses include strong recurring revenue and attractive returns-on-invested capital (ROIC). We have a strong orientation to low absolute and relative valuation, which are key to the execution of our investment strategy. A new idea will come from a variety of sources including company visits, screens, conferences, trade periodicals and general reading. All members of the research team are responsible for fundamental research.

Once an investment opportunity is identified it is put through an extensive due diligence process, which typically includes management interviews and site visits. When an acceptable level of conviction is achieved, the appropriate weighting (considering liquidity, valuation, etc.) is discussed and determined. A new company purchased in the portfolio will usually have an initial position size of 1-3%. The portfolio generally consists of 30-40 companies and is diversified across industries.

We are long-term investors, a typical holding period for our companies is three to five years, and portfolio turnover averages 20-40% per annum.
